summary: Joe Reis shares his experience speaking to his son's 6th-grade class about AI, offering practical advice for young people navigating a world increasingly shaped by artificial intelligence. The post combines this personal reflection with curated weekend reading links covering AI, tech, business, and upcoming events.
key_points: Spoke to 6th graders about AI, challenging their 'AI will take over the world' assumptions
Advised young people to reduce screen time, consider alternatives to college, and learn to work alongside AI
Curated links suggest AI hype might finally push companies to take data quality seriously
Expressed cautious agreement with Marc Andreessen's optimistic AI outlook while noting his tendency to pump investments
money_quotes: Get off your stupid phones and social media and interact with people in real life, consider alternatives to college (I'm a huge fan of apprenticeships), find a career that will be hard to automate, and learn to work and live alongside AI.

Lately, I've been chatting with people about how the rush to AI might (key word - might) be what companies need to take data quality seriously. Of course, I've been through a few other periods of ML/AI excitement, and it didn't happen then. So who the hell knows?
Or, make up a bunch of shit like a lot of 'AI' companies right now. Price the 'AI' using ChatGPT, an abacus, and a magic 8-Ball.
key_insight: Rather than fearing AI takeover, young people should focus on developing human skills, embracing change, and learning to work alongside AI as a tool.
